title: Projectile Vomiting - Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis
chief_complaint: 4-week-old firstborn male with progressive non-bilious projectile vomiting after every feed for 5 days. Hungry after vomiting. Weight loss of 200g. No diarrhea.

physical_exam.abdomen: Visible gastric peristalsis from left to right, palpable olive-shaped mass in right upper quadrant, no distension

hidden_data.cmp: Na: 130, K: 3.0, Cl: 86, HCO3: 32 (hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis)
hidden_data.us_abd: Hypertrophied pylorus, muscle thickness 4.5mm, length 18mm, no passage of gastric contents

key_learning_points: Non-bilious projectile vomiting in firstborn male at 3-6 weeks

patient_responses.vomiting: It shoots across the room after every feed
patient_responses.hunger: He wants to feed again right after vomiting
patient_responses.family: My husband had this as a baby too

feedback.incorrect_message: This is pyloric stenosis. The projectile vomiting + olive mass + ultrasound confirm it.
feedback.attending_note: Pyloric stenosis: Correct fluid and electrolyte abnormalities FIRST (NS + KCl), then pyloromyotomy. Do NOT operate until Cl >100 and HCO3 <30. Excellent prognosis after surgery. More common in firstborn males.
